one_line_takeaway: A training-free, prompt-only LLM 'sentinel' agent that purifies adversarial text inputs before classification, cutting attack success rate by ~29 points on average (up to 45.59%) on GLUE tasks with GPT-3.5 and LLAMA-2, though it remains brittle against few-shot and meaning-changing attacks.
key_finding: LLAMOS substantially reduces attack success rate without any target-model retraining. On GPT-3.5 across the six GLUE tasks it cut average ASR from 42.25% to 13.22% against PromptAttack-EN and from 48.81% to 19.42% against PromptAttack-FS-EN (~29 percentage-point average reductions), with a maximum single-task reduction of 37.86%. On LLAMA-2/SST-2 it cut PA-EN ASR from 66.77% to 21.18% (a 45.59% reduction, the paper's headline max) and PA-FS-EN from 48.39% to 37.82% (10.57%). The ICL refinement rescues the weak C3 case, lifting robust accuracy from 89.06% to 97.66% (C3) and 66.41% to 92.19% (C3-FS). The adversarial-system experiment shows neither agent wins outright: defense RA starts at 96.09%, drops to 56.25% after re-attack, and fluctuates over rounds, sometimes settling into an infinite loop where both agents regenerate identical sentences.
core_contribution: The paper introduces LLAMOS (Large LAnguage MOdel Sentinel), an LLM-based adversarial-purification defense that sits in front of a target LLM classifier. Rather than fine-tuning or retraining the target model (the AFT approach the authors position themselves against), LLAMOS uses a separate LLM as a 'defense agent' that rewrites potentially attacked textual inputs before they reach the target LLM. The defense agent is driven by two prompt components: (a) an Agent instruction that frames the task as producing a new sentence preserving the original meaning while altering minimal characters, and (b) a Defense guidance list of six concrete editing rules (e.g., modify as few characters as possible, correct spelling errors, eliminate redundant symbols). The paper adds two further contributions of its own: an in-context-learning (ICL) refinement that feeds attack-specific guidance to the defense agent to handle obvious attacks it initially misses, and an 'adversarial system' in which a defense agent and a purpose-built attack agent repeatedly counter each other around the target LLM. LLAMOS is presented as a plug-and-play, pre-processing module that requires no parameter updates to the target LLM.
threat_model: The adversary crafts adversarial textual examples against an LLM used as a classifier: small character-, word-, or sentence-level perturbations (typos, added trailing characters, synonym swaps, paraphrases, appended meaningless handles) that preserve human-perceived meaning but flip the target LLM's predicted label. The paper's concrete instantiation is PromptAttack, where an attacker LLM (fatk) is given a system prompt instructing it to generate a semantically-equivalent sentence classified as a different label. The defender controls a pre-processing agent but critically does NOT have access to the input's true label, making natural vs. adversarial inputs hard to distinguish. The paper explicitly notes failure cases: an attacker that changes actual meaning (e.g., inserting 'not') defeats purification, and a malicious system prompt embedded in the pipeline (e.g., appending ':)' before prediction) can also break the defense.
limitations: The defense is weakest against attacks that add obvious characters (C3 needed ICL to become effective) and against few-shot variants (PA-FS-EN reduction on LLAMA-2 was only 10.57%, and SST-2 residual ASR stayed at 37.82-48.94% in several settings). Because the defense agent lacks the input's ground-truth label, it cannot reliably tell natural from adversarial examples; meaning-changing attacks (inserting 'not') and malicious embedded system prompts defeat it entirely. The adversarial system can enter infinite loops with no resolution strategy offered. Evaluation is narrow: only two target models (GPT-3.5-Turbo, LLAMA-2-7b), only one attack family (PromptAttack), only GLUE classification tasks, and no comparison against other published defense baselines (only against no-defense). ICL guidance is manually tailored per attack, which undercuts the 'generalizes to unseen attacks' claim. Some in-text numbers are inconsistent with the tables (e.g., text cites a lowest RA of 86.96% while Table 4's lowest LLAMOS RA is 68.96%).

quantitative_results: GPT-3.5, PromptAttack-EN average ASR: 42.25% (no defense) -> 13.22% (LLAMOS), ~29.33 pt reduction
GPT-3.5, PromptAttack-FS-EN average ASR: 48.81% -> 19.42% (LLAMOS), ~29.39 pt reduction
GPT-3.5 maximum single-task ASR reduction: 37.86% (e.g., MNLI-mm PA-EN 43.51% -> 5.65%)
LLAMA-2 / SST-2, PA-EN ASR: 66.77% (Vanilla) -> 21.18% (LLAMOS), 45.59% reduction (paper headline)
LLAMA-2 / SST-2 standard accuracy 92.18%; drops to 30.42% under strong attack without defense; with LLAMOS robust accuracy 85.16% (character), 79.13% (word), 68.96% (sentence)
GPT-3.5 average robust accuracy: 43.49% (no defense, no FS) -> 70.03% (LLAMOS, no FS); Standard (clean) accuracy 76.43%
ICL on C3 attack robust accuracy: 89.06% -> 97.66%; C3 with FS: 66.41% -> 92.19%
Adversarial system robust accuracy over rounds: Defense 96.09/84.38/83.59/91.41/90.63%; Attack 56.25/43.53/45.93/50.34/28.13% (R1-R5)
baselines_compared: LLAMOS without ICL vs. LLAMOS with ICL (ablation)
Defense agent alone vs. defense agent within adversarial (attack+defense) system
Positioned conceptually against adversarial fine-tuning (AFT) / adversarial training, but not empirically compared to any external published defense
